title: Packages & pub.dev
objectives: Understand packages via a real-life analogy
Distinguish packages vs plugins vs federated plugins
Publish your own package to pub.dev

sections.markdown:
**Think of packages as ready-made LEGO kits at a hardware store.**

- Every Flutter app is built on top of dozens of packages. You COULD write your own HTTP client, your own state manager, your own image decoder — or you could grab a kit from **pub.dev** in one line of `pubspec.yaml`.
- **`dart` packages** — pure Dart code, no native side. Runs anywhere Dart runs. Example: `dio`, `intl`, `collection`.
- **`flutter` packages** — Dart code that depends on Flutter widgets/APIs but is still pure Dart. Example: `go_router`, `flutter_hooks`, `provider`.
- **`plugin` packages** — Dart PLUS platform-specific native code (Kotlin/Swift/etc.). They wrap platform channels or FFI. Example: `camera`, `geolocator`, `firebase_core`.
- **Federated plugins** — the plugin is split across MANY packages: `myplug` (interface), `myplug_android` (Android impl), `myplug_ios` (iOS impl). Lets third parties add platform support without touching the main package.

**Why this matters:** pub.dev is Flutter's economy. Reading pubspec, evaluating packages, and eventually publishing your own is table-stakes for a Flutter engineer. Smart Consumer already ships with 20+ transitive package dependencies — understanding this map is essential.

**A Flutter package** is a shareable unit of Dart/Flutter code hosted on **pub.dev**. Packages come in three flavors: **pure Dart** (no Flutter deps), **Flutter** (uses Flutter widgets/APIs), **plugin** (Dart + platform-native code via channels or FFI). Every package has a `pubspec.yaml` declaring name, version, dependencies, SDK constraints, and (for plugins) the native code paths per platform.
## `pubspec.yaml` — every line explained

sections.code:
name: se_learning_platform          # Your package name — must be snake_case, unique on pub.dev
description: >-                     # 60-180 char description (Pub Scoring uses this)
  An AI-powered Software Engineer learning platform for interview prep.
publish_to: 'none'                  # 'none' = never publish. Remove or set for pub.dev.
version: 1.0.0+1                    # semver + optional build number after `+`

repository: https://github.com/…    # Optional but boosts pub scoring
homepage: https://myapp.example     # Optional
issue_tracker: https://…            # Optional

environment:
  sdk: ^3.5.0                       # Dart SDK constraint
  flutter: '>=3.24.0'               # Flutter version constraint (packages using Flutter)

dependencies:                       # runtime deps — bundled into your app
  flutter:
    sdk: flutter                    # ← the Flutter framework itself
  http: ^1.2.2                      # Caret constraint: >=1.2.2 <2.0.0 (semver-major)
  go_router: ^14.6.2
  hive: ^2.2.3

dev_dependencies:                   # tools + tests, NOT shipped with the app
  flutter_lints: ^5.0.0
  build_runner: ^2.4.0
  freezed: ^2.5.0

dependency_overrides:               # emergency escape hatch — force a specific version
  # only use when you MUST pin a transitive dep
  http: 1.2.2

flutter:
  uses-material-design: true
  assets:
    - assets/images/
    - assets/content/roadmap.json
  fonts:
    - family: SFPro
      fonts:
        - asset: assets/fonts/SFPro-Regular.ttf
        - asset: assets/fonts/SFPro-Bold.ttf
          weight: 700
  # For plugins — declare platforms and their entry points:
  # plugin:
  #   platforms:
  #     android:
  #       package: com.myapp.myplugin
  #       pluginClass: MyPlugin
  #     ios:
  #       pluginClass: MyPlugin

sections.markdown: ## Version constraints — what the symbols mean

rows: Load from git URL / local path

sections.markdown: **The default `^` prefix follows semver.** `^1.2.3` = 'any version compatible with 1.2.3'. Semver says a MAJOR bump (1.x → 2.x) may break APIs; `^` prevents auto-upgrade to it. You get patch and minor updates for free (`pub upgrade`), major bumps are a conscious decision.
## Publishing a package to pub.dev

sections.code:
# 1. Scaffold a package (or plugin)
dart create --template=package my_awesome_pkg     # pure Dart
flutter create --template=package my_awesome_pkg  # Flutter package
flutter create --template=plugin \                # plugin with native code
  --platforms=android,ios,macos,windows,linux \
  --org=com.myapp my_awesome_plug

# 2. Write code + tests + docs

# 3. Verify metadata — pub.dev SCORES packages
flutter pub publish --dry-run
#    Fixes any warnings: missing description, missing license, missing example, etc.

# 4. Publish (login the first time; requires a Google account)
dart pub publish

# 5. Pub SCORE checks:
#      - Follows Dart conventions (analyzer warnings)
#      - Provides docs (README, dartdoc)
#      - Uses null safety
#      - Supports latest Dart/Flutter
#      - License declared (usually MIT/BSD/Apache-2.0)
#      - Has an example/ directory
#
#    Aim for the pub-dev score in the 130s+ (out of 150) — top ~10% of packages.

sections.markdown: ## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Adding to `dependencies:` what should be in `dev_dependencies:`.** Test / codegen / lint tools go under dev — they shouldn't ship with the app.
**Mistake 2: Pinning exact versions everywhere.** `http: 1.2.3` (no `^`) prevents auto-upgrades and creates dependency-hell when two packages disagree.
**Mistake 3: Ignoring `pub outdated`.** Run it every couple of weeks — surfaces packages with security fixes or bug fixes.
**Mistake 4: Adding heavy packages 'just in case'.** Every dep enters your APK. Check pub score, weekly downloads, last-updated date; abandoned packages become tech debt.
**Mistake 5: Publishing without a LICENSE + README + example.** Pub score plummets; users can't tell what your package does; nobody uses it.
## Best practices
**1. Use `^` version constraints.** Semver-compatible upgrades happen automatically.
**2. Vet packages before adding.** Score (Popularity / Likes / Points), last-updated date, maintainer, issue tracker health.
**3. Run `dart pub outdated` regularly.** Stay on current for security + performance.
**4. Prefer packages by 'verified publishers' on pub.dev.** Signed by Google or well-known orgs; a strong quality signal.
**5. Keep dev-only tools in `dev_dependencies`.** Reduces prod APK bloat.
**6. When publishing: LICENSE + README + example + tests + null-safety + Docs.** These are the top-scoring criteria.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Explore pubspec.yaml.** Open Smart Consumer's pubspec — identify every dep, dev-dep, asset, and constraint.
**Practice 2 (Easy) — pub outdated.** Run `flutter pub outdated`; upgrade one package; verify tests pass.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Scaffold a package.** `dart create --template=package my_math_pkg`; write a simple `add(int, int)` function; run tests.
**Practice 4 (Medium) — Local `path:` dep.** From another Flutter project, add `my_math_pkg` via `path: ../my_math_pkg`; use it.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Publish to pub.dev.** Write a small, useful package; ensure it scores 130+; publish; add its badge to your GitHub.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Federated plugin.** Split a plugin into interface + Android + iOS implementations; publish all three.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Package vs Plugin vs Federated Plugin?**

**A:** **Package** — pure Dart or Flutter Dart code; no native side. Examples: `intl`, `go_router`. Ships as source. **Plugin** — Dart AND platform-specific native code (Kotlin/Swift/etc.). Wraps platform channels or FFI. Examples: `camera`, `geolocator`. Ships Dart source + platform-native code per target. **Federated Plugin** — a plugin split across multiple packages so third parties can add platforms independently: `myplug` (Dart interface), `myplug_android` (Android impl), `myplug_ios` (iOS impl), `myplug_web` (web impl). Common in the Firebase / Google plugins. **Choose:** package if pure Dart, plugin if you need native APIs, federated plugin if you want an extensible platform-agnostic ecosystem.

**Q2: What is `pubspec.yaml`?**

**A:** A YAML file at every Flutter project's root that declares: **name / description / version** (semver), **`environment.sdk`** (Dart version), **`environment.flutter`** (Flutter version), **`dependencies`** (runtime — bundled with your app), **`dev_dependencies`** (build-time only — tests, codegen, lints), optional **`dependency_overrides`** (force a version), and the **`flutter:`** section (assets, fonts, plugin declarations). It's the single source of truth for what your app depends on and what ships. `pub get` / `flutter pub get` reads it, downloads packages, and writes `pubspec.lock` (which pins resolved versions).

**Q3: `^1.2.3` — what does the caret mean?**

**A:** '**Compatible with 1.2.3**' — any version >= 1.2.3 and < 2.0.0. Follows **semantic versioning**: MAJOR (2.0.0) is allowed to break APIs; MINOR / PATCH (1.3.0, 1.2.4) are backwards-compatible. `^` lets you get bug fixes and features for free while preventing surprise API breaks from a major bump. `flutter pub upgrade` respects `^` and pulls the latest compatible version. If you need to move to 2.x, you change the constraint deliberately. **This is the default** and almost always the right choice.

**Q4: `dependencies` vs `dev_dependencies` — what goes where?**

**A:** **`dependencies`** — runtime deps bundled with the app. HTTP client, state manager, JSON serializer, DB, plugins. These are what your app IMPORTS and needs at runtime. **`dev_dependencies`** — build-time only, NOT shipped with the app. Testing frameworks (`flutter_test`, `mockito`), code generators (`build_runner`, `freezed`, `json_serializable`), lints (`flutter_lints`), local test utilities. **The distinction matters:** dev-deps don't bloat your APK; also, downstream users of your package don't get transitively burdened by dev-only packages. **Rule:** if it's needed to RUN the app, `dependencies`. If it's needed to DEVELOP / TEST / GENERATE code, `dev_dependencies`.

**Q5: How do you evaluate a package before adding it?**

**A:** On pub.dev, check the four pillars: **(1) Score (Points / 150)** — measures null safety, docs, example, license, dependencies. Aim for 130+. **(2) Popularity** — download rank; more usage = more real-world testing. **(3) Likes** — community sentiment. **(4) Verified Publisher** badge — signed by an organization, adds trust. Also: **last-updated date** (if > 1 year old and Flutter has moved, be cautious), **issues** (open bug count + response rate on GitHub), **maintainer's track record**, **example/ folder present**. **Rule:** for a critical dep, spend 10 minutes vetting — abandoned packages become tech debt years later. `dio` vs an unmaintained fork isn't a hard choice.

**Q6: How do you publish your own package to pub.dev?**

**A:** **(1) Scaffold:** `dart create --template=package my_pkg` (pure Dart) or `flutter create --template=plugin --platforms=android,ios my_plug`. **(2) Write:** implement your Dart API, add tests, write a README, add an `example/` app, declare a LICENSE (MIT/BSD/Apache-2.0). **(3) Verify:** `flutter pub publish --dry-run` — surfaces every scoring issue. Fix them. **(4) Publish:** `dart pub publish`. First time asks you to log in with a Google account. **(5) Post-publish:** monitor pub score, respond to issues, ship updates. **The pub-dev score** rewards: null safety, up-to-date SDK, complete metadata, example folder, no analyzer warnings, no unpublished dependency-overrides. **Rule:** aim for 130+ points; anything less signals a quick-and-dirty package.

**Q7: How do you deal with a dependency you can't upgrade?**

**A:** Two situations. **(1) A transitive dep** conflicts between two of YOUR dependencies. Fix: figure out which top-level dep pulls the outdated version (`pub deps`), upgrade THAT one to a version compatible with the newer transitive. If impossible, use `dependency_overrides:` to force a version (emergency escape hatch). **(2) YOUR direct dep** has a critical bug or a breaking API change. Fix: fork on GitHub, apply a patch, use `git:` in pubspec (`http: git: https://github.com/…`) pointing to your fork; open a PR upstream so you can revert to the pub version once merged. **Rule:** avoid `dependency_overrides:` unless truly stuck — it's a maintenance smell.

## Real-world usage

- **Smart Consumer's `pubspec.yaml`** — likely `dio`, `provider`/`bloc`, `intl`, `flutter_svg`, `image_picker`, and 15 more.
- **`pub.dev`** — the central package registry; every serious Flutter app depends on it.
- **Verified publishers** — `google.dev`, `firebase.google.com`, `bloclibrary.dev`, `riverpod.dev` — high-trust badges.
- **Enterprise private packages** — companies host internal packages via git URLs or self-hosted registries (`pub_hosted`).
- **`melos`** — tool for managing multi-package Dart repositories (a monorepo of packages).

## Advanced notes

- **`pubspec.lock`** — pins resolved versions; commit it for apps, DON'T commit it for library packages.
- **`.pub-cache`** — Dart's package cache. Clear with `dart pub cache clean` if a corrupt package is suspected.
- **`pub_hosted`** — self-host a private pub registry.
- **`melos`** — monorepo management for Dart / Flutter packages.
- **`workspaces:` field (pubspec)** — Dart 3.5+ supports pubspec workspaces (multiple packages sharing one lock file).
- **`dart pub deps`** — inspect the dependency graph.
- **`dart pub upgrade --major-versions`** — bump majors (with care).
- **`dart pub outdated`** — report outdated packages.
- **`flutter pub run <tool>`** — execute a package's binary from your project.
- **`.pubignore`** — files to skip when publishing (like `.gitignore` for pub.dev).

flashcards.back: Package = pure Dart/Flutter. Plugin = also has native Kotlin/Swift/etc. code.
Split across interface + per-platform implementation packages so third parties can add platforms.
>=1.2.3 <2.0.0 — semver-compatible upgrades free; blocks breaking major bumps.
dependencies = runtime, bundled. dev_dependencies = build/test/gen tools; NOT in the app.
Score, popularity, likes, verified publisher, last-updated, issue health.
Scaffold → LICENSE + README + example → pub publish --dry-run → fix warnings → dart pub publish.

quiz.explain: Test tool, not shipped with app.

challenges.text: Read Smart Consumer's pubspec — list every runtime + dev dep.
Add flutter_lints in dev_dependencies; enable them; fix warnings.
Run flutter pub outdated; upgrade one dep; verify tests.
Scaffold `my_pkg` with `dart create --template=package`; write a helper + tests.
Import `my_pkg` from another project via `path:` dep; use its API.
Publish a small package to pub.dev; aim for 130+ score.
Build a federated plugin — interface + Android + iOS implementations, all publishable.
